Skelly-Belvieu is a 568-mile, 8-inch common carrier pipeline regulated by the
Commission under the Interstate Commerce Act. Skelly-Belvieu states that it is a
50150 joint venture between Enterprise Operating Products, LLC (Enterprise), the operator of the pipeline, and WesTTex 66 Pipeline Company. Skelly-Belvieu transports hatched NGLs from Skellytown, Texas to the fractional hub at Mont Belvieu. SkellyBelvieu explains that in recent years the supply ofNGLs at Skellytown has increased significantly, creating a need for additional capacity to reach downstream markets.

The pipeline transports a mix of butane and propane and runs from Enterprise’s Mid-America pipeline that is fractionated at the Hobbs facility. BL: Our Refined Products assets consists of a common carrier refined products pipeline system and two marine storage terminals. We own the longest common carrier pipeline system for refined products in the United States, extending approximately 9,800 miles from the Texas Gulf Coast and covering a 15-state area across the central U.S. The system includes 47 million barrels of aggregate usable storage capacity at 54 connected terminals.
Our Galena Park marine terminal is located along the Houston Ship Channel and has 13 million barrels of wholly owned storage capacity and 1 million barrels of storage capacity owned through a joint venture. The Pasadena marine terminal, also owned through a joint venture, is also located along the Houston Ship Channel and has a storage capacity of 5 million barrels.

column22: Iraqi government refers to this as the 'third offshore pipeline at Grand Faw Port,' counting the two existing ICOEEP sealines (already in tracker) as the first and second. EPC contract signed 13 Apr 2025 between Basra Oil Company and Esta Construction (Turkey) + Micoperi (Italy) consortium. 757-day construction period implies completion approx. late 2027. Design capacity 2.4 mmbpd; operational capacity reported as >2 mmbpd. Connects Al-Faw peninsula storage to ABOT, Khor al-Amaya (KAAOT), and a new floating export platform (SPM-4, 900,000 bpd). Also includes two offshore platforms (VS-1, VS-2) and 60 km dual marine power/communications cable. Cost not yet disclosed publicly.
column23: Iraq launched construction of a new offshore export pipeline at Grand Faw Port on 13 April 2025, when Basra Oil Company signed an EPC contract with a Turkish-Italian consortium of Esta Construction and Micoperi. The project involves a 70 km pipeline (9 km onshore, 61 km offshore, 48-inch diameter), two offshore valve station platforms (VS-1 and VS-2), a floating export buoy rated at 900,000 bpd (SPM-4), and a 60 km dual marine power and communications cable. Design capacity is 2.4 mmbpd. Deputy PM and Oil Minister Hayan Abdul-Ghani described it as 'one of the most important strategic undertakings' in Iraq's export network. The project will be completed in three stages: reactivating Khor al-Amaya; linking the Basra terminal; and installing the floating export platform. Phase 1 completion targeted within two years of signing (~Apr 2027).

column23: In 2017, the Dongying Development and Reform Commission proposed the Dongying–Guangrao Oil Pipeline project. The project is owned by Dongying Haixin Storage Co., Ltd. The pipeline runs from Dongying Port to Guangrao and consists of two segments: the Dongying–Haojia Segment and the Haojia–Guangrao Segment. Both segments are designed with reverse-flow capability.The Dongying–Haojia Segment is a crude oil/naphtha pipeline with a designed throughput capacity of 16 mtpa, including 7 mtpa of crude oil and 9 mtpa of naphtha. Construction started on 26 October 2018 and was completed on 31 August 2023. This project entered the trial operation period on 15 September 2024. The trial operation period runs from 15 September 2024 to 14 September 2025.The Haojia–Guangrao Segment is also a crude oil/naphtha pipeline, with a designed throughput capacity of 20 mtpa, comprising 11 mtpa of crude oil and 9 mtpa of naphtha. Construction began in June 2024, and the segment was put into operation on 8 December 2025.

The Dongying–Haojia Segment is a crude oil/naphtha pipeline with a designed throughput capacity of 16 mtpa, including 7 mtpa of crude oil and 9 mtpa of naphtha. Construction started on 26 October 2018 and was completed on 31 August 2023. This project entered the trial operation period on 15 September 2024. The trial operation period runs from 15 September 2024 to 14 September 2025.
The Haojia–Guangrao Segment is also a crude oil/naphtha pipeline, with a designed throughput capacity of 20 mtpa, comprising 11 mtpa of crude oil and 9 mtpa of naphtha. Construction began in June 2024, and the segment was put into operation on 8 December 2025.

column23: The pipeline starts at Binzhou Port in the Beihai New Area of Binzhou, Shandong Province, and terminates at the Xinyue Chemical Plant in Chengkou Town, Wudi County, Binzhou. It passes through the Beihai New Area and Wudi County.Construction of the pipeline began in February 2023. The pipeline has a total length of approximately 62.6 km, including 60.6 km from the Binzhou Port pumping station to the Xinyue pumping station and a 2 km section from the Xinyue station to the Xinyue tank farm. The crude oil and diesel pipelines are laid in parallel. On 15 March 2023, the crude oil pipeline was officially commissioned.In December 2025, the Binzhou–Xinyue Oil Pipeline completed its first in-line inspection.

On 31 October 2018, the Dongying Municipal Development and Reform Commission approved Dongying Port - Guangrao Oil Pipeline (North-South Oil Pipeline West Line). The project is a supporting facility for the 250,000-ton crude oil import berth at Dongying Port and includes three crude oil pipelines: Dongying Port -Lique Oil Pipeline One, Dongying Port-Dezhou Oil Pipeline, and Dongying Port-Shouguang Oil Pipeline.
On 8 July 2021, the Dongying Port - Guangrao Oil Pipeline (North-South Oil Pipeline West Line) was listed as a key energy project in the Outline of the 14th Five-Year Plan for National Economic and Social Development of Kenli District, Dongying City, and the Long-Range Objectives Through 2035, issued by the People’s Government of Kenli District. On 15 April 2025, the Fourth Session of the Ninth Dongying Municipal Committee of the CPPCC proposed the construction of the project

column23:
In June 2023, the Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) report for the project was released.
The total investment of the project is RMB 33.5 million. The project plans to construct eight pipelines, including two condensate pipelines and one crude oil pipeline. Phase I commenced on 30 August 2023, was completed on 20 May 2025, and underwent commissioning from 23 May to 22 August 2025.
Condensate Pipeline One from Haixin Storage to Shenchi Storage is not yet completed.
Condensate Pipeline Two from Shenchi Storage to Gangbei 1st Road has been completed.
Crude Oil Pipeline from the crude oil tank farm of Shandong Baogang International Port Co., Ltd. to Shenchi Storage has been completed.

The total investment of the project is RMB 33.5 million. The project plans to construct eight pipelines, including two condensate pipelines and one crude oil pipeline. Phase I commenced on 30 August 2023, was completed on 20 May 2025, and underwent commissioning from 23 May to 22 August 2025.
Condensate Pipeline One from Haixin Storage to Shenchi Storage is not yet completed.
Condensate Pipeline Two from Shenchi Storage to Gangbei 1st Road has been completed.
Crude Oil Pipeline from the crude oil tank farm of Shandong Baogang International Port Co., Ltd. to Shenchi Storage has been completed.

column23:
On 28 December 2021, the project was included as a key oil and gas project in the "14th Five-Year Comprehensive Transportation Development Plan of Liaoning Province."
On 21 October 2024, construction of the project officially commenced
The project forms part of the Northeast Land–Sea New Corridor and comprises two parallel pipelines, Huajin Oil Pipeline (Jinzhou Port-Panjin) and Beili Oil Pipeline (Jinzhou Port-Panjin) . The pipelines are laid in a common trench from the Jinzhou Port Oil Pumping Station to the north side of Huajin Group’s old plant within the Panjin Fine Chemical Industry Park, with a co-laying length of 126.5 km. Huajin Oil Pipeline (Jinzhou Port-Panjin) terminates at the Panjin Oil Pumping Station, while Beili Oil Pipeline (Jinzhou Port-Panjin) terminates at the Beili Oil Pumping Station.
21 October 2025, Section 1 of the project has been completed.

column22: Gregor, Jan 2025: The unnamed project announced by Oldelval's CEO in late 2023 appears to be the same project described elsewhere as Proyecto Triplicar, so I have adjusted the name accordingly. "Triplicar" refers to the fact that the Oldelval network's original capacity of 36,000 m3/day would be at least tripled, to a minimum of 108,000 m3/day. As originally described, the capacity expansion would be achieved through upgrades to existing pumping stations rather than by the addition of new pipeline segments. Capacity estimates have ranged widely, as noted on the wiki page. Amalia, Jan 2024: Unnamed project so far, from what I can tell; there are mentions of a project called the Proyecto Triplicar which might be the same, but no confirmation so far, so leaving it as unnamed for the time being
column23: New expansion project was recently announced in November 29, 2023. The project will increase the network's capacity by an extra 300,000 barrels per day, to increase basin dispatch capacity from the Neuquén basin to 1,000,000 barrels per day. Oldeval's CEO announced the project during an Energy Day event.

column22: Gregor, Jan 2025: The original plan for this project involved construction of a new pipeline parallel to Oldelval's existing Puesto Hernández-Medanito pipeline, but flowing in the opposite direction, from Medanito to Puesto Hernández. It's clear that efforts to "reverse the flow" between Puesto Hernández and Medanito got underway in October 2021, but it's hard to tell whether this means reversal of the existing pipeline or initial development efforts on the new, parallel pipeline. I can find no conclusive indication that oil has begun flowing from Medanito to Puesto Hernández via the old pipeline or that construction of a new segment has actually begun, so I'm keeping status as "Proposed" for now. This deserves further scrutiny in the next update. Amalia: Project appears to comprise both the flow reversal of an existing pipeline and the construction of a parallel pipeline to increase capacity

column23: The Trans-Arabian Pipeline (Tapline) was a crude oil pipeline running from Qaisumah in Saudi Arabia across Jordan, southern Syria (including the Golan Heights), and into Lebanon, terminating at the Sidon (Zahrani) Mediterranean export terminal. Constructed 1947-1950 by the Trans-Arabian Pipeline Company (a subsidiary of Aramco) using 30-inch and 31-inch steel pipe over a 1,213-1,214 km route, with six main pumping stations (Nariya, Qaisumah, Rafha, Badanah, Turaif in Saudi Arabia; Qaryatain in Jordan). Initial capacity 320,000 bpd, expanded via auxiliary pumping in 1957 to ~450,000 bpd and a peak nameplate of ~500,000 bpd. The cross-border line ceased operating in 1976 as the Lebanese Civil War began and supertankers eroded its economics; the Saudi and Jordanian portions continued intermittently until 1983/1990. Final 'de-cruding' (cleaning of residual crude) was completed in 2001. Designated Saudi Arabia's first industrial heritage site in December 2020.

column23: The Qatif-Qaisumah gathering line is the 507 km / 315 mi Saudi-internal segment of the Trans-Arabian Pipeline system. It gathered crude from the Qatif and Abqaiq field areas on the Persian Gulf coast of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province and delivered it to Qaisumah, where the main 1,213 km Tapline mainline began its run northwest across Jordan, Syria, and Lebanon to the Sidon Mediterranean terminal. Built 1947-1950 alongside the mainline by the Trans-Arabian Pipeline Company (Aramco subsidiary). Operations wound down through the 1980s and ceased in 1990; final de-cruding 2001.

column9: Expand Shaybah-Abqaiq Pipeline Capacity; Petroline loop segment
column22: RECLASSIFIED from Shaybah-Abqaiq Oil Pipeline Expansion. C.A.T. Group lists project location as 'KSA – Riyadh area – East/West Corridor', placing it on the Petroline (East-West Pipeline) route, not the Shaybah-Abqaiq desert route. The 42-inch diameter and Riyadh-area location are inconsistent with the 30-inch SHBAB-2 desert pipeline. This segment expands downstream crude throughput capacity for Shaybah crude after it reaches Abqaiq and enters the Petroline system.
column23: C.A.T. Group (est. 1937) built this 42-inch, 220-km pipeline segment as an EPC-LSTK project to expand crude oil throughput capacity on the East-West Pipeline (Petroline) corridor. The project is named 'Expand Shaybah-Abqaiq Pipeline Capacity' by the contractor, reflecting Aramco's program-level objective of increasing total system capacity for Shaybah crude from field to export terminal. Scope included 2 launcher/receiver areas, 5 mainline valve stations, and 6 surge relief skids. C.A.T. has extensive history on the Petroline corridor, having previously completed a 536-km, 48-inch East-West crude oil pipeline segment.

column22: 30-inch crude oil feeder pipeline connecting the new GOSP-4 at Shaybah to the existing 46-inch SHBAB-1 mainline. This is a partial-length segment (217 km) running within the Rub' al-Khali desert, not the full 645-km Shaybah-Abqaiq distance. Built by Russia's Stroytransgaz (first-ever Saudi Aramco contract for the company). OGJ reports welding completed June 2008; full commissioning ~2009-2010. Added 50% to existing pipeline capacity, supporting the field expansion from 500,000 to 750,000 bpd. Scope included 5 valve units with surge-relief skids, launching/receiving stations, leak detection, cathodic protection, plus improvements to 21 existing units on the SHBAB-1 line. Note: AramcoLife (Oct 2021) describes the full system as having 46-inch and 30-inch parallel lines stretching 645 km, suggesting the 30-inch line was extended beyond this 217-km segment in subsequent expansion phases. MEED references a pre-existing '480-km loop line', hinting at additional 30-inch segments not covered by this record.
column23: Stroytransgaz signed the EPC contract on March 31, 2007, for approximately $100 million. The 217-km, 30-inch pipeline connects GOSP-4 to the existing SHBAB-1 crude pipeline system within the Shaybah field area. Welding was completed by June 2008 at rates of up to 2 km/day, with four temporary worker villages built along the desert route. Full project completion was delayed from June 2008 to late 2009/early 2010. The pipeline supported Phase 1 expansion of Shaybah from 500,000 to 750,000 bpd of Arabian Extra Light crude.
